title: A resolution expressing the sense of the Senate that the United States, States, cities, Tribal nations, businesses, institutions of higher education, and other institutions in the United States should work toward achieving the goals of the Paris Agreement.
plain_summary: This Senate resolution says the United States should stay part of the Paris Agreement, which is a deal between countries to fight climate change. The resolution explains that climate change is causing problems like extreme weather and rising sea levels. It says the US government, states, cities, tribal nations, businesses, and schools should all work together to reduce pollution that causes climate change. The resolution also mentions that fighting climate change can create jobs and help the economy while using cleaner energy.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Foreign Relations. (text: CR S372-373)

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution expresses what the Senate believes about climate change. The Senate thinks that the United States government at all levels, along with states, cities, tribal nations, businesses, colleges, and other organizations should work together to reach the goals of the Paris Agreement.

sections.title: Background Facts
sections.text: One hundred ninety-five out of one hundred ninety-eight countries that signed the United Nations climate treaty have agreed to follow the Paris Agreement. This agreement was made in Paris, France on December twelfth, two thousand fifteen. Scientists who study climate change found that human activities have been the main cause of climate change over the past one hundred years. Human-caused climate change has led to widespread and fast changes in the air, oceans, ice, and living things on Earth. Communities that did the least to cause climate change are hurt the most by its effects. The harmful effects of climate change will keep getting worse. If we keep producing greenhouse gases, all parts of the climate system will be affected more, and extreme weather will become more severe. Global warming will likely reach one and a half degrees Celsius above pre-industrial levels even if we reduce greenhouse gas emissions. Climate change is already causing economic damage in areas like farming, forests, fishing, energy, and tourism. To avoid the worst effects of climate change, global temperatures must stay below one and a half degrees Celsius above pre-industrial levels. To limit warming to one and a half degrees Celsius, we need rapid, deep, and immediate cuts in greenhouse gas emissions. Taking strong action between two thousand twenty and two thousand thirty would help reduce harm to humans and nature. In two thousand twenty-four, the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration reported twenty-seven disasters that each caused at least one billion dollars in damage. The total damage was about one hundred eighty-two billion seven hundred million dollars. NASA found that greenhouse gas emissions in the United States went down in two thousand twenty because of the economic recession caused by the coronavirus pandemic. In two thousand twenty-one and two thousand twenty-two, carbon dioxide emissions from burning fossil fuels in the United States went up eight percent compared to two thousand twenty and one percent compared to two thousand twenty-one. This brought emissions back to pre-pandemic levels. In two thousand twenty-two, renewable energy produced more electricity than coal for the first time in the United States. In two thousand twenty-three, about forty percent of the world's electricity came from zero-carbon sources. In two thousand twenty-four, car companies sold more than one million three hundred thousand electric vehicles in the United States. This was eight percent of all new car sales. Thirty-two states have made climate plans. Twenty-nine states and the District of Columbia have adopted renewable portfolio standards, which require utilities to get a certain amount of their electricity from renewable sources. Thirty-six states and the District of Columbia have adopted clean vehicle policies. Twenty-four states and the District of Columbia have set greenhouse gas emission targets. Thirty-three states have adopted energy efficiency resource standards. Eleven states have joined the Regional Greenhouse Gas Initiative, which creates a market system that limits emissions from electric power plants. The cap on emissions goes down by three percent each year from two thousand twenty-one through two thousand thirty. The goal is to reduce power sector emissions by thirty percent below two thousand twenty levels by two thousand thirty. California has a plan to reduce greenhouse gas emissions to forty-eight percent below nineteen ninety levels by two thousand thirty and reduce emissions by eighty-five percent by two thousand forty-five. In the United States, ninety cities, eleven counties, two states, and the District of Columbia have set goals to use one hundred percent clean and renewable energy. Two hundred seventeen companies have committed to one hundred percent renewable energy. Since the Inflation Reduction Act was passed in August two thousand twenty-two, clean energy companies have announced or started nearly seven hundred fifty projects. This represents more than four hundred twenty-two billion dollars in investments and has created more than four hundred thousand new clean energy jobs. More than eighty-five percent of the investments from the Inflation Reduction Act were made in counties with below average college graduation rates. More than seventy-five percent of investments were made in areas with below average household incomes. The Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act and the Inflation Reduction Act are expected to create up to one million seven hundred thousand new jobs by two thousand thirty and two million nine hundred thousand jobs by two thousand thirty-five. In two thousand twenty-four, the United States submitted a new plan to the Paris Agreement to reduce greenhouse gas emissions by sixty-one to sixty-six percent below two thousand five levels by two thousand thirty-five. This is possible partly because of programs and investments from the Inflation Reduction Act and the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act. In two thousand twenty-three, more money was invested in solar energy than in oil for the first time worldwide. In two thousand twenty-three, more than eight million three hundred thousand people in the United States worked in the energy sector in all fifty states. This includes jobs in wind energy, solar energy, energy efficiency, clean vehicles, and energy storage. About four hundred ninety-five thousand eight hundred seventy-one people worked in the solar and wind industries, including roofers, electricians, and steel workers. The Department of Energy found that new clean energy jobs are growing more than two times faster than other energy jobs and the overall United States economy. New vehicle emissions standards updated by the Environmental Protection Agency for cars made from two thousand twenty-seven through two thousand thirty-two are predicted to provide thirteen billion dollars in annual health benefits from cleaner air, save drivers nearly six thousand dollars over the life of a new vehicle from fuel and maintenance costs, and prevent twenty-five thousand early deaths. The America Is All In coalition started from the We Are Still In Coalition launched in two thousand seventeen. It has become the largest local climate coalition in the United States. It includes states, tribal nations, cities, businesses, universities, healthcare organizations, faith groups, and cultural institutions. The coalition has committed to uphold the Paris Agreement and represents about three-fourths of the United States gross domestic product and two-thirds of the United States population through city and state partners. The coalition supports local climate leaders as they build climate resilience and sustainable supply chains. On the day before President Donald Trump announced the United States withdrawal from the Paris Agreement on June first, two thousand seventeen, a spokesperson from China's Ministry of Foreign Affairs said that China would stay committed to supporting global climate action. China is currently the world's largest producer of greenhouse gas emissions. According to the International Energy Agency, China has made more than eight hundred billion dollars in foreign investment in clean energy and energy storage around the world since two thousand sixteen. The United States needs both a fully engaged federal government and states, cities, businesses, and all local groups working together to reduce emissions, avoid the worst effects of climate change, and compete in the global clean energy market.

sections.title: Senate Resolution
sections.text: The Senate believes that the United States should remain a party to the Paris Agreement. The United States should support policies at the federal, state, and local levels that help reduce global warming pollution and aim to meet the goals of the Paris Agreement. The United States should support the clear intentions and efforts of businesses, investors, and all of American society to take action on climate change.
